---
title: "XGL screen garbled after ATI driver update"
date: 2007-07-14
forum: Desktop Effects &amp; Customization
---

### Post by AdrianOfTyriel on 2007-07-14
Hi, i updated my ATI drivers, but now can no longer log in to my XGL session because the screen gets all garbled. I can however, log in to Gnome, and thus have maintained the use of my computer. I've tried re-installing Compiz-Fusion, but it doesn't help, same problem.

Here is my xorg.conf:
```

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf(5) manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen 0 "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
	Inputdevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	Inputdevice	"Configured Mouse"
	Inputdevice	"stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Inputdevice	"cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Inputdevice	"eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "Files"
	
	# path to defoma fonts
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/misc"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/cyrillic"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi/:unscaled"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi/:unscaled"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/Type1"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi"
	Fontpath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load		"i2c"
	Load		"bitmap"
	Load		"ddc"
	Load		"dri"
	Load		"extmod"
	Load		"freetype"
	Load		"glx"
	Load		"int10"
	Load		"vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"	"ImPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"	"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"stylus"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"stylus"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"eraser"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"eraser"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"cursor"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"cursor"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Generic Monitor"
	Horizsync	28.0	-	64.0
	Vertrefresh	43.0	-	60.0
	Option		"DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	Option		"VendorName"	"ATI Proprietary Driver"
	Option		"ModelName"	"Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
	Option		"DPMS"	"true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Generic Video Card"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	Busid		"PCI:2:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	Option		"VideoOverlay"	"on"
	Option		"OpenGLOverlay"	"off"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"Generic Video Card"
	Monitor		"Generic Monitor"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	1
		Modes		"1280x1024"	"1280x960"	"1280x854"	"1280x800"	"1280x768"	"1200x800"	"1152x864"	"1152x768"	"1024x768"	"800x600"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection

	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	15

	EndSubSection
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth	16
		Modes		"1280x1024"	"1280x960"	"1280x854"	"1280x800"	"1280x768"	"1200x800"	"1152x864"	"1152x768"	"1024x768"	"800x600"	"640x480"
	EndSubSection

	Identifier	"aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device		"aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor		"aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport	0	0
		Depth	24
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option		"Composite"	"0"
EndSection
```

Maybe there's more you need to see, but forgive me, i'm new to ubuntu, and am feeling a bit like a fish out of water when i run into these issues.

### Post by grahams on 2007-07-16
Hi

I had exactly the same issue with 8.38.6. It is surprising such a large company released a driver update which broken so many users. However I reverted back to the previous release and all is well again. 

As it's not open source there is nothing to do apart from email AMD/ATI and wait for the next bug fix.

BTW. any one know if/when AMD is follow up on their claim of making their driver open source.

```
# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf(5) manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/misc"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/cyrillic"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/Type1"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi"
	# path to defoma fonts

EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load	"i2c"
	Load	"bitmap"
	Load	"ddc"
	Load	"dri"
	Load	"extmod"
	Load	"freetype"
	Load	"glx"

EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"		"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"		"ImPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"		"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"stylus"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"		"stylus"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"eraser"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"		"eraser"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Identifier	"cursor"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"		"cursor"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"Generic Video Card"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option		"Composite"	"0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"Generic Monitor"
	Option		"DPMS"
	HorizSync	28-64
	VertRefresh	43-60
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Device		"Generic Video Card"
	Monitor		"Generic Monitor"
	DefaultDepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Depth		1
		Modes		"1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
```

If they arent there then uninstall and get the latest drivers because there is something wrong.

### Post by sili on 2007-07-23
Well, I registered just to post this, I hope it'll helps someone else but me

I've got a pretty beefy system, the videocard isn't the newest but still holds its own with newer games.

Videocard is an ATI Radeon X1600Pro 512MB AGP (sapphire brand), don't ask why I need agp. In any case, I cannot run beryl without turning my agp arpeture size in the bios down to 64 Megs or so (my lowest setting). I discovered this, not as a fluke, but after exhaustive reading on the subject because I REALLY had to get it running to play with it, i'm sure some of you understand. Mandriva which comes with a stable version of compiz built in ran fine for me, but no luck with anything else, including beryl. After about 2-3 months of spending every off hour I've had trying to find a solution, I translated a foreign language forum page I'd found through google which wasnt the same card but the exact same problem.

If you're trying everything including updating drivers and trying new kernels give this a shot, it only takes a reboot. This may not help anyone, in which case good luck.

---

### Post by Bohlio on 2007-07-23

### Post by Orestis_G on 2007-07-25
Hello everyone,

I had the exact same problem, until I stumbled into the solution while searching the Net:

[http://www.stanchina.net/~flavio/debian/fglrx-archive/msg00788.html](http://www.stanchina.net/~flavio/debian/fglrx-archive/msg00788.html)

For the lazy ones among you, this is the option, which has to be added to the Device section:

Option "KernelModuleParm"           "agplock=0"

---

### Post by Ber P on 2007-07-30
I have the exact same problem with my new 9600xt. Tried Orestis_G suggestion with the Option "KernelModuleParm" "agplock=0"  and it worked! - partly anyway. Now XGL starts up, and the screen looks right, BUT it is painfully slow. 
I've confirmed that XGL is running and that it is using fglrx. But I've lost direct rendering and it also makes a complain about OpenGL . Here is the output:

$ glxinfo | grep -i opengl
Xlib:  extension "XFree86-DRI" missing on display ":1.0".
OpenGL vendor string: ATI Technologies Inc.
OpenGL renderer string: ATI RADEON 9600 Series
OpenGL version string: 1.2 (2.0.6474 (8.38.7))
OpenGL extensions:

and 

$ glxinfo | grep -i direct
Xlib:  extension "XFree86-DRI" missing on display ":1.0".
direct rendering: No

Well - one step forward and .......

### Post by mobad on 2007-07-30
OK, this should be the solution to everyones problem...
Just replace your Xorg.conf with this one...

```

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf(5) manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier	"Default Layout"
  screen 0 "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
	Inputdevice	"Generic Keyboard"
	Inputdevice	"Configured Mouse"
	Inputdevice	"stylus"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Inputdevice	"cursor"	"SendCoreEvents"
	Inputdevice	"eraser"	"SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

Section "Files"
	
	# path to defoma fonts
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/misc"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/cyrillic"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi/:unscaled"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi/:unscaled"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/Type1"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi"
	Fontpath	"/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi"
	Fontpath	"/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load		"i2c"
	Load		"bitmap"
	Load		"ddc"
	Load		"dri"
	Load		"extmod"
	Load		"freetype"
	Load		"glx"
	Load		"int10"
	Load		"vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Generic Keyboard"
	Driver		"kbd"
	Option		"CoreKeyboard"
	Option		"XkbRules"	"xorg"
	Option		"XkbModel"	"pc105"
	Option		"XkbLayout"	"us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"Configured Mouse"
	Driver		"mouse"
	Option		"CorePointer"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/mice"
	Option		"Protocol"	"ImPS/2"
	Option		"ZAxisMapping"	"4 5"
	Option		"Emulate3Buttons"	"true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"stylus"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"stylus"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"eraser"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"eraser"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier	"cursor"
	Driver		"wacom"
	Option		"Device"	"/dev/input/wacom"
	Option		"Type"	"cursor"
	Option		"ForceDevice"	"ISDV4"# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier	"aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	Option		"VendorName"	"ATI Proprietary Driver"
	Option		"ModelName"	"Generic Autodetecting Monitor"
	Option		"DPMS"	"true"
EndSection

Section "Device"
	Identifier	"aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Driver		"fglrx"
	Option		"VideoOverlay"	"on"
	Option		"OpenGLOverlay"	"off"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"aticonfig-Screen[0]"
	Device		"aticonfig-Device[0]"
	Monitor		"aticonfig-Monitor[0]"
	Defaultdepth	24
	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport	0	0
		Depth	24
	EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "DRI"
	Mode	0666
EndSection

Section "Extensions"
	Option		"Composite"	"0"
EndSection

```

### Post by mobad on 2007-07-30
jsully1 you probably had the same problem as me. 

I could get the Ubuntu fglrx working fine but once I installed 8.39.4 or any other version I either got no direct rendering or I got two Xorg's running.  I fixed this problem though... it is a bit hacked up.

OK, there are two ways you can deal with this problem:

First solution is that you just go back to the regular Ubuntu fglrx this is the option I recommend because there are almost no benefits to the newer drivers.

The second solution is that you install 8.39.4 AND the Ubuntu fglrx I get direct rendering but I get two Xorg's running which can slow/crash your computer.  
Now I'm not sure if everyone has this problem or if its just me (Feisty 64bit with radeon X700) but there is a simple way to test.

Simply run "ps -A | grep Xorg" in the terminal and if you get two Xorg results you have this problem.

Now my fix for this problem is just not worth it in my opinion but I will post it anyway.
First create a file called killxorg
Put the line below inside the file and put your password where it says to (THIS CAN COMPROMISE THE SECURITY OF YOUR COMPUTER use the first solution)

echo "your password here" | sudo -S kill `ps -d | grep -w Xorg | cut -c1-6`

Save, then "chmod +x /path/to/killxorg" after that make this script start up at each login.

OK I think thats it, oh and for anyone that was wondering how that xorg.conf works, it's really simple.  
The not working xorg.conf had two of everything.  It had two Monitor sections, it had two Device sections and it had two Screen sections so to fix it you simply have to remove one of each.

### Post by schnupi on 2007-08-01
well here it is folks... its working for me now..and the solution was right infront of my/our eyes..
it fixed my mesa/open gl problem and its all working now


its in the official ubuntu how to .. [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BinaryDriverHowto/ATI#head-d01742cec183112be090e459b74129606e258f79](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/BinaryDriverHowto/ATI#head-d01742cec183112be090e459b74129606e258f79)

for the lazy ones
> 
If fglrxinfo gives you the following, your installation is not completed correctly:
[quote]
$ fglrxinfo 
display: :0.0  screen: 0
OpenGL vendor string: Mesa project: [www.mesa3d.org](www.mesa3d.org)
OpenGL renderer string: Mesa GLX Indirect
OpenGL version string: 1.2 (1.5 Mesa 6.4.1)

These two commands might fix your installation, try this, reboot, and run fglrxinfo again:
> 
sudo mkdir -p /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/dri  
sudo ln -s /usr/lib/dri/fglrx_dri.so /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/dri 

[/quote]

and just in case here is my xorg.conf(i've a ati x700)

> 

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the xorg.conf(5) manual page.
# (Type "man xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
	Identifier     "Default Layout"
	Screen      0  "aticonfig-Screen[0]" 0 0
	InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
	InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
	InputDevice    "stylus" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice    "cursor" "SendCoreEvents"
	InputDevice    "eraser" "SendCoreEvents"
	Option	    "AIGLX" "true"
EndSection

Section "Files"

	# path to defoma fonts
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/misc"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/cyrillic"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi/:unscaled"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/Type1"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/100dpi"
	FontPath     "/usr/share/fonts/X11/75dpi"
	FontPath     "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
EndSection

Section "Module"
	Load  "i2c"
	Load  "bitmap"
	Load  "ddc"
	Load  "dri"
	Load  "extmod"
	Load  "freetype"
	Load  "glx"
	Load  "int10"
	Load  "vbe"
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
	Option	    "AIGLX" "off"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Generic Keyboard"
	Driver      "kbd"
	Option	    "CoreKeyboard"
	Option	    "XkbRules" "xorg"
	Option	    "XkbModel" "pc105"
	Option	    "XkbLayout" "de"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "Configured Mouse"
	Driver      "mouse"
	Option	    "CorePointer"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
	Option	    "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
	Option	    "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
	Option	    "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "stylus"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"

	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "eraser"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
	Identifier  "cursor"
	Driver      "wacom"
	Option	    "Device" "/dev/input/wacom"
	Option	    "Type" "cursor"
	Option	    "ForceDevice" "ISDV4"		# Tablet PC ONLY
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
	Identifier   "Generic Monitor"
	HorizSync    28.0 - 51.0
	VertRefresh  43.0 - 60.0
	Option	    "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"

#	DisplaySize	338	203	# 1280x768 96dpi
#	DisplaySize	338	254	# 1280x960 96dpi
	# 1280x800 96dpi

	EndSubSection

EndSection

	SubSection "Display"
		Viewport   0 0
		Depth     24
	EndSubSection
EndSection

	Mode         0666
EndSection

	Option	    "Composite" "Disable"
EndSection

### Post by mrmiserable on 2007-08-02
dont know if this helps but i always delete all ati fglrx stuff before updating 
secondly xgl does not do dri i think
i always compile new ati driver with this method and beryl always works great 
using dell 9100 ati x600 
also when compiling do this in gnome not xgl i found this to give problems
change to driver in use now obviously eg  (8.39.4)
also thank to the people who wrote these guides

I have had problems with the fglrx drivers after 8.32.5, but now its working with my system and I wanted to share how. This is my first howto, so suggestions are welcome.

Here are the steps I took to get the driver working Ubuntu Feisty 7.04 and Xorg 7.2.

If you have previously installed a restricted driver, you need to disable it by adding fglrx to DISABLED_MODULES in /etc/default/linux-restricted-modules-common

*You need to enable the universe repository before updating

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y --force-yes module-assistant build-essential fakeroot dh-make debhelper debconf libstdc++5 linux-headers-$(uname -r) wget

Download ati proprietary driver
cd ~/
sudo wget [https://a248.e.akamai.net/f/674/9206/0/www2.ati.com/drivers/linux/ati-driver-installer-8.32.5-x86.x86_64.run](https://a248.e.akamai.net/f/674/9206/0/www2.ati.com/drivers/linux/ati-driver-installer-8.32.5-x86.x86_64.run)

Build .deb packages

sudo sh ./ati-driver-installer-8.38.6-x86.x86_64.run --buildpkg Ubuntu/edgy

Install .deb packages:

sudo dpkg -i xorg-driver-fglrx*.deb
sudo dpkg -i fglrx-kernel-source*.deb
sudo dpkg -i fglrx-control*.deb

Remove any old fglrx debs from /usr/src/:

sudo rm /usr/src/fglrx-kernel*.deb

Needed for 8.32.5

sudo mkdir -p /usr/src/modules/fglrx/linux
sudo touch /usr/src/modules/fglrx/linux/config.h

Download Patch for 2.6.20.* kernels

cd ~/
sudo wget [http://darcs.frugalware.org/repos/frugalware-current/source/x11-extra/fglrx/fglrx-2.6.20.patch](http://darcs.frugalware.org/repos/frugalware-current/source/x11-extra/fglrx/fglrx-2.6.20.patch)

Apply Patch for 2.6.20.* kernels

cd /usr/src
sudo cp fglrx.tar.bz2 fglrx.tar.bz2-original
sudo tar -xvjf fglrx.tar.bz2
cd /usr/src/modules/fglrx
sudo patch < ~/fglrx-2.6.20.patch
cd /usr/src
sudo tar -cvjf fglrx.tar.bz2 modules/fglrx

Compile the kernel module:

sudo module-assistant prepare

sudo module-assistant update

sudo module-assistant build fglrx

sudo module-assistant install fglrx

sudo depmod -a

Initial ati setup

If you prefer to edit your /etc/X11/xorg.conf manually, just replace the "vesa", "ati", or "radeon" with "fglrx" instead of running the command below

sudo aticonfig --initial

sudo aticonfig --overlay-type=Xv

You'll also want to disable AIGLX and Composite by adding this to your /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Code:

Section "Extensions"
	Option	    "Composite" "Disable"
EndSection

Section "ServerFlags"
	Option	    "AIGLX" "off"
EndSection

And reboot your machine.

After rebooting, you can run

Code:

fglrxinfo

Which should show something like this

Code:

display: :0.0  screen: 0
OpenGL vendor string: ATI Technologies Inc.
OpenGL renderer string: RADEON XPRESS Series Generic
OpenGL version string: 2.0.6234 (8.32.5)

From here you should be able to follow the steps to install Xgl and Beryl

[http://wiki.beryl-project.org/wiki/I...eisty_with_XGL](http://wiki.beryl-project.org/wiki/I...eisty_with_XGL)

I hope this helps someone

### Post by Orestis_G on 2007-08-26
Ok, guys, here is a last one from me:

Assuming you have downloaded and built the packages for the system:

e.g. sudo sh ati-driver-installer-8.40.4-x86.x86_64.run --buildpkg Ubuntu/7.04

After you install the deb packages e.g. sudo dpkg -i *.deb, you have to build the modules for the current kernel/driver combination with sudo module-assistant build fglrx ; sudo module-assistant install fglrx, since the modules are not built automatically on package installation.

I would first suggest that you go to /usr/src and delete any old fglrx module packages which may have been left over from the previous fglrx installation.

After that, add the option I have stated in my previous posts to the xorg.conf file and reboot. The glx server should be working properly now, along with beryl. However, when the glx server is running, direct rendering is indeed off. Someone with a working knowledge of the graphics subsystem could perhaps give us some more insight on that, but as far as I know, direct rendering works only with the standard X server.

### Post by Tiger-Smith on 2007-08-29
Right as I know of and tested this works on all ATi cards that are supported by drivers from ATi, go to their website [www.ati.com](www.ati.com) and download the driver that corresponds with your card store it on your desktop for ease, then press ALT+F1 login as you normally would in terminal then type cd Desktop, then type dir and remeber the ati installers name, after this type sudo bash ./ati-driver-installer-8.40.4-x86.x86_64.run or whatever version yours is then type your password for the sudo value 
let it install thats step one

next step would after its finished reboot then log back in go to system administration restricted drivers manager and enable it it will install xorg drivers once its finished press ALT+F1 again and repeat step one to patch mesa drivers with ati fglrx drivers once its done reboot and log on again go to the terminal and type fglrxinfo if it shows ati then well done if not, then dont blame me for smashing your pc in rage...

Tygah

### Post by Jouke74 on 2007-08-31
For all the people running XGL and looking at "Mesa" driver info after fglrxinfo here is the following explanation:

- I assume you all installed the FGLRX ATI driver (version does not matter).
- If you log into a normal non-XGL session Fglrxinfo will show that you are using an ATI card and that direct rendering (DRI) is available. 
- If you log into a XGL session and type Fglrxinfo it will show that you are running on "mesa" drivers and that DRI is not available. 

This is because of the following:
When your system boots it will load stuff into the following order.

1. First the Fglrx driver with it's settings in Xorg.

2. Then the X-server which is the basic graphics frame server on which all your programs give output to screen 0. Here direct acceleration for the ATI driver is available for individual programs and you can play games.

Since your working on screen 0, when fglrxinfo is loaded it will show FGLRX driver and that DRI can be run. 

Without XGL:

(layer 1)
Scr0+FGLRX
Xserver

Window2
Prg1-DRI
Window2
Window1
Prg2+DRI
Prg2+DRI
Window1


>>>

Now if you log into a XGL session the following is happening:

Steps 1 and 2 are the same.

3. Subsequently the XGL server program is started on screen 1. This program catches all graphics (windows / gnome etc) from screen 0 and turns it into a single full screen window (the one you see). This is accelerated and send to the Opengl part of the video card which will show it on screen 1. Since the whole window is completely accelerated (and composite able) it is not possible anymore for the individual programs that run on the XGL-server to determine their acceleration (that's why it is turned of, ATI's fault). Also the XGL server is using a Mesa library and drivers to make it work. As a consequence, if you type "fglrxinfo" under the XGL server, it will assume screen 1 and not 0, and the program will only catch the layer of the XGL-server and will report "Mesa" drivers. 

However, in the background your FGLRX drivers are working on the invisible screen 0. And they are working hard with DRI to nicely accelerate your whole XGL server. It will just not show. 

Some people are replacing the mesa libraries of the XGL server to use the ATI driver's libraries. I guess it will cause a lot of instability and conflict since these are not made to do what XGL server does. 

With XGL:
(layer 1) > (layer 2)
Scr0+FGLRX > Scr1+MESA
Xserver > XGL-server

Window2 > Window1+DRI
Prg1-DRI > Prg-DRI
Window2 >
Window1  >
Prg2+DRI > Prg-DRI
Prg2+DRI > Prg-DRI
Window1 > Window1+DRI

How to get rid of this: Buy a Nvidia card... or live with it.

Another option is to exchange your X-server with the XGL server. This will still not enable DRI, however programs are straight drawn on layer 1, which saves some calculations.

---
